suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Mike Mulligan and His Steam Shovel
suggested.author: Virginia Lee Burton

competence_markers.description: Does the child contribute reliably to group efforts?
Can they follow another child's lead with good faith and effort?
Do they communicate problems rather than hiding or withdrawing?
Do they help others without being prompted?
Can they participate in after-action discussions about what worked and what did not?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you were part of a group that worked well together.
narration_prompts.detail: What made it work? What did each person do? How did it feel?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you had to follow someone else's lead.
narration_prompts.detail: What did they ask you to do? Did you do it? Was it hard to follow instead of lead?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you noticed someone in your group needed help and you helped without being asked.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you notice? What did you do?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time a group project did not go well.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? Why did it not work? What could have been different?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why is it important to say something when you are stuck or when you see a problem?
narration_prompts.detail: What happens if you do not say anything?

cross_connections.description: Personal reliability is the prerequisite for team contribution
Self-control enables the cooperation and patience teamwork requires
Clear communication is the tool that makes coordination possible
Team friction naturally produces disagreements that require resolution
The interpersonal skills that make teamwork pleasant and productive

content.why_this_matters:
Almost everything worth doing requires working with other people. Building something big. Solving a hard problem. Running a household. Accomplishing a mission. You cannot do these things alone — not because you are not smart or capable, but because one person has limits that a group does not.

But here is what most people never learn: putting good people in the same room does not automatically produce good teamwork. Working together is a skill. It has to be learned, practiced, and developed — just like reading or math or building things with your hands.

This packet teaches you the most basic and most important teamwork skills: sharing a task, following someone else's lead, communicating when something is wrong, helping without being asked, and experiencing what it feels like when a group succeeds together — and what it feels like when a group effort falls apart.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Sharing a task** — dividing work fairly and doing your part fully
2. **Following direction** — executing someone else's plan with discipline and good faith
3. **Communicating needs and problems** — telling the group when something is not working
4. **Helping without being asked** — noticing when a teammate needs assistance and offering it
5. **Experiencing group success and group failure** — learning from both what coordination produces and what its absence costs
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Your Part Matters

When a group is working on something together, every person has a part. Maybe your part is gathering the materials. Maybe your part is building one section. Maybe your part is cleaning up. Whatever your part is, the group depends on you to do it — all of it, on time, to the standard.

This is different from doing something just for yourself. When you are working alone and you do a sloppy job, the only person affected is you. When you are working with others and you do a sloppy job, everyone is affected. The group project is only as strong as its weakest part. If you do not finish your section, there is a hole. If you do your section carelessly, the whole thing looks careless.

That is a big responsibility. It is also what makes teamwork meaningful. When you do your part well, and the other people do their parts well, and it all comes together — that feeling is different from anything you can accomplish alone. You made something together. Each person's contribution mattered. And the result is bigger and better than what any one person could have produced.

The practice starts simple: when you are given a part of a group task, do it fully. Not halfway. Not "good enough." Fully. If you are not sure what "fully" means, ask. If you finish early and your part is truly done, look around — someone else might need help with theirs.

### Part 2: Following Someone Else's Lead

Sometimes you are in charge. Sometimes someone else is in charge. Both roles are important, and both take skill.

When someone else is leading — an older student, a peer, a mentor — your job is to do what they ask with full effort and good faith. This does not mean you cannot ask questions. "Why are we doing it this way?" is a good question. "I do not understand what you want me to do" is an important thing to say. But "I do not want to do it that way" or "my idea was better" — when the leader has already decided — is not helpful. It slows the group down and makes coordination harder.

Following well is not the same as being passive. A good follower executes the plan, but they also pay attention. If they see a problem the leader has not noticed, they say something: "I think we might run out of time for this part" or "this is not working the way we expected." That is not challenging the leader. That is helping the leader by providing information they need.

The hardest part of following is doing it when you disagree with the decision. You wanted to build it differently. You think your idea was better. Maybe it was. But the leader chose a different approach, and now the team needs to execute that approach. You can share your concern once, respectfully. Then you commit. If the approach fails, the after-action review is the place to discuss alternatives — not during execution when everyone needs to be pulling in the same direction.

### Part 3: Saying What Needs to Be Said

One of the most important things a team member can do is speak up when something is wrong. Not complain. Not blame. Just say, clearly and simply, what is happening.

"I am stuck and I need help." "This part is not going to be finished in time." "I think there is a problem with the plan." "I made a mistake on my section."

These sentences are hard to say. They feel like admitting weakness or failure. But in a team, hiding problems is far worse than admitting them. When you hide a problem, it grows. By the time someone else notices, it may be too late to fix. When you surface a problem early, the team can adjust. Maybe someone helps you. Maybe the plan changes. Maybe the timeline shifts. But the team knows the truth, and they can respond to reality instead of being surprised by it.

The opposite of surfacing problems is suffering in silence. The team member who is stuck but says nothing, who is falling behind but pretends everything is fine, who sees a problem but assumes someone else will notice — that person is not being brave. They are being harmful, even though they do not intend to be.

The practice: when something is wrong, say so. As soon as you notice it. Use simple, specific words. Then work with the team to address it.

### Part 4: Helping Without Being Asked

Look around during a group task. Is anyone struggling? Is anyone's section behind? Are there things that need doing that nobody has been assigned?

If the answer to any of these is yes, and you are in a position to help — help. Do not wait to be asked. Do not wait for the leader to notice and assign it. Just do it.

This is the team version of initiative, and it is one of the things that separates a group of individuals from an actual team. In a group of individuals, each person focuses only on their assigned part and ignores everything else. In a team, each person is aware of the whole — they know what needs to happen for the group to succeed, and they act on that awareness.

Helping without being asked does not mean taking over someone else's work. It means offering assistance: "Do you need a hand with that?" "I am done with my part — can I help with yours?" "I noticed this is not set up yet — want me to do it?" The offer gives the other person the choice to accept or decline. But the offer itself demonstrates that you are paying attention to the team, not just to yourself.

### Part 5: When It Works and When It Does Not

You will experience both. A group project that comes together beautifully — everyone does their part, the coordination is smooth, the result is something no one could have done alone. And a group project that falls apart — someone does not finish, the plan was unclear, nobody was really coordinating, and the result is frustrating for everyone.

Both experiences are valuable. The success teaches you what good coordination feels like — and that it does not happen by accident. Someone was communicating clearly. Someone was checking in. Everyone was doing their part. The failure teaches you what happens when coordination breaks down — and it gives you something to analyze. What went wrong? Was the plan unclear? Did someone not do their part? Did no one check in? Did people hide problems instead of surfacing them?

After every group experience, take a few minutes to talk about what happened. Not to blame. To understand. "What worked?" "What did not work?" "What would we do differently next time?" This conversation — even at your age, even about a simple project — builds the habit of learning from group experience. It is the same after-action review that military teams, sports teams, and business teams use. The habit starts now.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Reliable Contribution
**Developing well:** The child consistently completes their part of group tasks fully and on time. Other children know they can count on this child.

**Developing slowly:** The child frequently does not finish their part, does it carelessly, or needs constant reminding. The group compensates for their unreliability.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child's assigned part appropriate for their skill level? Are they clear on what "done" looks like?

### 2. Followership
**Developing well:** The child can execute another child's plan with effort and good faith, even when they would have chosen differently. They ask clarifying questions and provide helpful feedback without undermining.

**Developing slowly:** The child resists following, becomes sulky or passive when not leading, or undermines the leader's plan. They refuse to cooperate unless their idea is chosen.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the child been explicitly taught that following is a skill, not a lesser role? Is the child given genuine leadership opportunities so that following does not feel like permanent subordination?

### 3. Problem Communication
**Developing well:** The child speaks up when they are stuck, when they see a problem, or when something is not working. They use specific language and do so early.

**Developing slowly:** The child hides problems, suffers silently, or waits until the situation has deteriorated before saying anything. Alternatively, they complain without being specific about the actual problem.

**Diagnostic questions:** How does the group (and you) respond when a child surfaces a problem? If the response is blame or frustration, children learn to stay silent. If the response is "thank you for telling us — let us fix this," they learn to speak up.
